title: Pre recorded Lecture Part C Slides
body:
Dr Linh Nguyen, Course Convenor Lecturer UNSW Centre for Social Impact Email: linh-nguyen@unsw.edu.au  Observation Survey Interview

▪ Observational research involves systematically observing and recording behavior, events, or phenomena as they occur in their natural settings (Liedtka 2019; Siu 2021) ▪ A valuable approach for gaining insights into real-world phenomena that may be difficult to capture through other research methods (Liedtka 2019). ▪ Observation data can be collected through personal observations, electronic observations, or trace analysis (Siu 2021) Image: Twenty Interior

Participant observation: The researcher becomes a part of the group or setting being studied, actively participating and observing from within.

Non-participant observation: The researcher remains a detached observer, watching and recording without direct involvement.

Structured observation: Observations are guided by a predetermined set of categories or behaviors to observe.

Unstructured observation: Observations are more open-ended, allowing for the exploration of unexpected or emerging phenomena.

Source: Creswell and Poth 2016 5  Advantages Limitations and Challenges

▪ Provides a direct and firsthand account ▪ Observer bias: The presence or of behavior in natural settings. characteristics of the observer may influence the observed behavior or ▪ Allows for the discovery of unanticipated situation. or counterintuitive findings that may be missed through other methods. ▪ Difficulty in capturing the complete ▪ Avoids reliance on self-reported data, context or underlying motivations behind which can be subject to biases or observed behavior. inaccuracies. ▪ Access & Ethical concerns: Observational research may raise privacy or access issues in certain sensitive settings.

Source: Based on Liedtka (2019); Siu (2021) 6   1 2 3 4 5

Clearly define the Select the appropriate Develop an observation Determine the sampling Decide on the data research question and type of observation guide or protocol to approach (e.g., purposive, collection methods (e.g., objectives to guide the based on the research ensure consistency and convenience, random) for field notes, audio/video observation process. goals and practical focus during selecting the participants recordings) to capture considerations. observations. or settings to observe. and document observations.

Source: Based on Creswell and Poth (2016); Liedtka (2019) 7  Some findings from observation of retail channels ▪ Our group conducted fieldwork in the Sydney suburb of Bankstown where we sampled 56 products from 10 retail channels, ▪ Our field research in Bankstown confirmed that the 300–750ml bottle size range was most popular among both customers and restaurant owners. Some findings that could emerge from observational research in the retail store context The observers noted that many customers appeared confused or overwhelmed when first entering the store, wandering aimlessly instead of heading straight for desired sections. This could indicate a need for better wayfinding or organizational flow. At the dressing rooms, researchers documented long lines and customers juggling multiple item options, suggesting an opportunity to increase the number of rooms or provide mobile checkout to reduce hassle. In the men's department, very few shoppers actually tried on clothes before purchasing, perhaps indicating a lack of appealing試着rooms or desire for a quicker self-service model. Observers saw several customers take photos of themselves in outfits and show friends/family, revealing the importance of creating Instagram-worthy moments and environments. At checkout, the friction of long lines and customers digging through purses/wallets for payment emerged as a major pain point that could potentially be solved through mobile checkout or automated systems.

How to structure a Survey? Before you start: • Identify Key Information Needs: Determine the essential information required to provide reliable recommendations and minimize the client's risk of failure in this market. • Which of these questions would be best addressed in a survey? Which one is the most important (“If we don’t have this information….”)? • Define the purpose of the survey(s): What do you hope to learn from the survey? • Define the target group: who, where, when, how

1. What DATA do you need to get the INFORMATION / INSIGHT you are looking for? 2. For each question, ask yourselves • Is this really relevant? • Can the respondent misinterpret the question? • Would I be able to answer the question? • What is the best way to ask this? Multiple choice, matrix, yes/no, open end, one or more answers, etc. • Do we capture every possible answer (incl. “don’t know” “n.a.” or multiple answers if relevant) • Can the data collected in this way, be turned into useful information? 3. Keep it short and to the point (10 Questions max) 4. Introduction: Include an introduction that states the purpose of the survey and the time it will take for your participants in clear and engaging language 5. Closing: Consider “Would you be willing to leave your contact details etc.” & “Thank you!”  Example

Fish sauce preferences and purchasing habits

Dear respondents,

We are …. We are doing a survey for a project in ... The project aims to assist a local Thai fish sauce producer in exploring opportunities to introduce their products to Australia. The purpose of this survey is to understand consumer preferences and purchasing habits of fish sauce in Australia, with a particular interest in fish sauce of Thai origin. Fish sauce is a traditional Thai condiment made from fermented anchovies and salt. It is commonly used as a dipping sauce and cooking condiment. This survey will take approximately 3-5 minutes to complete. This survey is anonymous and all information collected will be kept at the highest level of confidentiality. Thank you for your time and support!

• Multiple choice questions • Rating scale questions • Likert scale questions • Matrix questions • Dropdown questions • Open-ended questions • Demographic questions • Ranking questions • Image choice question

Single select multiple choice Multi select multiple choice questions questions

https://www.questionpro.com;  Source: https://www.surveymonkey.com/  ▪ Qualtrics | UNSW Research ▪ https://www.surveymonkey.com/mp/survey-questiontypes/#multiple-choice ▪ https://www.questionpro.com/article/multiple-choicequestions.html#What_Are_Multiple_Choice_Questions  1.Identify Key Interviewees: Determine who can provide valuable insights for your research. 2.Early Outreach: Begin contacting potential interviewees as soon as possible. 3.Sample Email Template: Subject: Request for Interview - [Project Name] Dear [Name], I'm conducting action research for [Project]. Would you be available for a brief discussion about [Specific Topic]? Your insights will significantly impact our strategy development for [Goal/Outcome]. Thank you for considering this request. I look forward to the possibility of speaking with you Kind regards [Your Name]

Source: Constable (2014)   • Introduce team/yourself Introduction • Introduce topic/project • Agreements re recording / confidentiality / time

• Questions Main • Probing interview • Recaps

• Recommendations who else to contact? Closing • Questions / comments? • Thank you

1. Put the other person at ease 2. Prepare well: what would you like to get out of the interview? 3. Search for the unexpected 4. Others: • Make sure your questions are CLEAR (practice: how would you answer?) Avoid jargon! • Prepare OPEN questions (you only need a few CLOSED questions) • Try to build RAPORT with the person you interview • LISTEN attentively, RESPOND and PROBE (= ‘tell-me-more’ questions) • PARAPHRASE (= ‘so if I understand you well, you are saying….?’) • Be prepared to open up yourself AND know when to say nothing! • Learn how to STEER the interview and CHANGE THE TOPIC when appropriate • REMEMBER what has been said earlier on!

Which stakeholders do we need to speak with? Why? • Subject matter expertise • Grounded perspective on the problem's context • Diverse perspectives

• Are we asking the right questions? • Do our questions explore the topics we need knowledge on?

• As you discuss your individual research conducted in your groups, you will begin to see a pattern emerge • What new knowledge have we acquired? • Where do we need to go from here?

▪ Have a conversation and encourage stories ▪ Don’t suggest answers to your questions ▪ Don’t be afraid of silence ▪ Look for inconsistencies ▪ Be aware of nonverbal cues ▪ Stay on the same path of a question Image: KnowledgeCity ▪ Keep Asking “Why?” ▪ Remember to record key quotes and observations

Based on Bogner, Littig, and Menz (2009); Van Audenhove and Donders (2019)  Perspective taking

Staying out of judgement

Recognizing emotion in other people

Communicating that ‘emotion in other people’  Source: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1Evwgu369Jw  ▪ Look for unspoken cues. Hesitation, body language, faces, grunts. ▪ Find out underlying causes, motivations, drivers. “Why did you have to ask the boss?” ▪ Ask for examples, e.g. “What’s an example of red tape?” ▪ Ask for details, e.g. “What specifically made it annoying?” ▪ Ask what they did/felt, e.g. “What was your reaction to that?” ▪ “What else?” (open, implied) ▪ Check for bias. “Is that normal?” “What’s another example?” ▪ Don’t put words in their mouth. Use their words to clarify. ▪ Listen more, talk less. Let them fill uncomfortable silences.  MOM TEST: This is a good example of HOW TO ASK YOUR INTERVIEW QUESTIONS

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Hla1jzhan78&t=10s  ▪ Would it be OK to follow up with you again when we are a bit further down the track? (Get contact details if relevant/possible) ▪ Who else do you think I should interview about this? (Could you introduce us?)  1. Not using the right tool for the right purpose 2. Not asking the right questions 3. Not formulating your question well 4. Open versus closed questions (Interview versus Survey) 5. Not asking the right people 6. One size fits all: no segmentation questions 7. Sample size and distribution, e.g. N=1 (“I know”; my father told me…)  ▪ Know what you need to know ▪ Ask the right questions ▪ Always start with what is already known (fast and cheap!) ▪ Use the right method for additional data-collection ▪ Structure, question and reflect to move from data to information to insight   ▪ Angrosino, MV 2007, Doing ethnographic and observational research, SAGE, Los Angeles ▪ Bogner, A., Littig, B. and Menz, W., 2009. Introduction: Expert interviews—An introduction to a new methodological debate. In Interviewing experts (pp. 1-13). London: Palgrave Macmillan UK. ▪ Constable, G 2014, Talking to Humans: Success starts with understanding your customers, Kindle Edition. ▪ Liedtka, J, Ogilvie, T & Brozenske, R 2019, The designing for growth field book : a step-by-step project guide Second edition., Columbia Business School Publishing, Columbia University Press, New York. ▪ Siu, B 2021, What is observational research?, SAGE Publications, Incorporated, Los Angeles, CA. ▪ Van Audenhove, L. and Donders, K., 2019. Talking to people III: Expert interviews and elite interviews. The Palgrave handbook of methods for media policy research, pp.179-197. ▪ RSA, 2013. Brené Brown on Empathy. [video] YouTube.
